key_insight: Surface wrinkling in a compressed hyperelastic solid can be reinterpreted as a topological phase transition, with mechanical stretch tuning a Dirac-like mass and triggering a change in winding number.
engineering_relevance: This framework could enable predictive design of soft surfaces whose instability, localization, and patterning are programmed through geometry, loading, and material choice rather than treated as failure alone.
method: Generalized Stroh formalism with Lie-group metric evolution to capture finite-strain geometric frustration.
Map the surface impedance matrix to an effective one-dimensional Dirac Hamiltonian.
Use winding-number topology to identify the onset of wrinkles and the associated zero-energy edge state.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports an analytical mapping for a semi-infinite hyperelastic half-space under finite compression, showing a quantized winding-number jump at the wrinkling onset.
watch_item: The result is presented analytically for an idealized semi-infinite hyperelastic half-space, so validation under real material heterogeneity, finite thickness, and boundary imperfections will be important.
paper_title: Elastic Surface Instability as a Topological Phase Transition
